speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Kate Price Grannis took place at her home in Lewiston on February 24, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager... Tell us that your folks came to live at Avon, where they came from and how they got to Avon. Well, no. I haven't made any notes at all until just a few minutes ago. Don't worry about notes. I want to hear from you.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: In about 1880, a certain parcel of the land up in Latah County, Idaho, was thrown open for people to take homes, make homes of them. My folks at that time were living, well, they were out of Spokane. Here's where I fall down. I forget the places. Out towards Cheney. They were building a railroad there, up that way. My father was head charge of a group of Chinaman, but that's just this railroad work. They found out this land was thrown open for settlement there. A great bunch of people from there that had gotten acquainted, they'd come from different parts of the east. My folks were from California, my father and my mother's folks were from Kansas. They were at Cheney, that's where the work was then. When this land was thrown open, then a bunch of them went up and located there. They were acquainted with each other. Now, the Hawkins, the Lunsfords, and some of those people. That's how Avon Country got started, or Latah County.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they were acquainted with each other from the area near Cheney, where they were working?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, where they were working. My father and mother were married from Cheney. My grandfather and folks had come there too. They had come from Kansas, and my father had come from California. They got acquainted at Cheney. My father and mother were married there. Other friends too of the group that was there were married in that group and came up to Avon together. That's very interesting because you often get the idea that people came out by themselves and it seems more often to be the way you're describing it. It came by knowing other folks. Well, that's it. They knew my mother waited table in a restaurant there and a lady by the name of Dolly Parks, which belongs to the Lawrence bunch up there. My mother was waiting table and this Dolly Parks came in and all she had was, all the clothing she had was tied up in the bandana handkerchief. She did the laundry work and cleaning up, and my mother waited table. So that's the way we got acquainted. Before that, the fellow that married Dolly Parks came in a train of people from California. Some of those people, they divided in two trains. One train started to go ahead and we said, we're going on. The rear part of the train said, well, we're going to stay. Those that went on were massacred on the way up. Mr. Cole's father was killed, but Mr. Cole happened to stay back with the other train. I've heard my father tell that many times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your father in either of those parties?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: He was with one of the wagon trains, yes, coming up from California then. That's before they met and settled out in the Cheney Country.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: My father was born in California.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: His family and the others that were in Cheney, they were actually settled there and were farming in that country as well?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, they were working, yes. They had places and were working. I don't think any of them owned farmland there. But there was work because there was a railroad being built there. That was largely railroad work. Yes, that was the big thing then, building that railroad from Spokane to Cheney and right in there somewhere. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father ever talk about, or your mother talk about why they came to the Cheney area and why they left California or Kansas? There wasn't the opportunity there perhaps?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, you know, an awful lot of people wanted to come west. And I have a book of Savannah, where my folks came from and settled in California.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From Savannah, Georgia?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Not Georgia, Savannah, Illinois. Savannah, Illinois. Savannah, Illinois. I couldn't think of what it was. But they came, my father's grandfather was one of the men that founded the town of Savannah, Illinois. And there was quite a number came west in the train at the same time. And my mother's father and those, they didn't know each other until they met at Cheney, in the Cheney country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when they learned about the land open around Avon, did they come over and move in permanently and stake homesteads out then? Is that what they did? Were they some of the first, very first people in that area? In Avon?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes. Yes, some of the very first people that came to that part of the country. I don't say the very first. I don't know who was, but I know we all knew the Lunceford's and the Parkses and the Hawkinses and some of them came from Kansas and then this one train of people that came up, my father was with, from California.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever talk about being boss over the Chinese?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh yes, he used to laugh about it. And he'd say some things in Chinese, he'd learn some of the words and say them. And when he got elderly, he'd say some of those words and they weren't good words at all. You know, you always hear the worst.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he think of, was it difficult for him?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: He couldn't speak Chinese. Oh no, no. He had a bunch of men under him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have anything to say about how they were as workmen?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: There was a lot of, a lot of people didn't care for the Chinese. That's it, they didn't care for them. They didn't think they, they didn't value them very much. They think very highly of them at all. They're kind of, I don't know, I wouldn't, I couldn't express this how they felt, but. Well, I've just heard that they. Well, like slaves, nearly. But they just had crews of men, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know, have any idea what they were looking for? What they, like what your parents wanted on their piece of land? What kind of a place they were trying to stake out?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Why they, why they picked the. Just employment. At that time it was employment.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I don't, I don't think they had, I know they didn't have any, I mustn't say they didn't have any holdings in the Cheney Country. I don't, I don't know. I don't believe they did. Because I don't think they gave up anything, only just their labor there to come up here to. But that was labor in those days, you see, that was their livelihood. But then it must have been pretty rough starting in at Avon with nothing there and there wasn't any industry there when they started. It was. And everybody helped everybody else. If there was a building, the country, in the Avon country in those days, was heavily timbered with black pine. And a lot of people, well, a good many of them, hewed out the felled -o's trees and skinned those, the bark all off and made log houses of them. I've seen some of those men with, they had broad axes, what they call broad axes. Had a blade about like this, big heavy axe, and they squared those logs. And then lost them at the corner to put up their homes.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: And when there was a new barn or something built, everybody came for a barn -raising or something like that, you know.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I take it that just about nothing was being done with money. I mean, it was probably mostly all exchange work. Well, that's it. That's it. Everybody helped everybody else in early days. When we finally settled there, my mother's family, my mother's family had, well I think that's what I want to say. Kind of left me what I was going to say. Well, we were talking about helping, about how people help each other. Well, everybody did help each other if they wanted to raise a building, you know. And by, they just, the whole neighborhood came women and all bring something to eat and have a big time and maybe put up the sides of a barn or something, or a cabin.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I was a, I wasn't there at that time, in fact. But I heard my daddy tell about some of those early days. The Greenwoods now, my grandfathers, were there ahead of us. And my father's folks who had come from Cali, who had come from Savannah, Illinois to,

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: words leave me, to California and then on up here.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Can't say what I want to say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, don't they bother you?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, it's, I just am trying to think what I want to do in that sentence. I want to complete. Well, you said that you, they, your parents came after your grandparents. They were here first a little before. Yes. And you were born in Avon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, how long after your parents came were you born?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: You were born in 88,

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Then my folks moved back up into the Riordan country and were there a while and then back to Avon again and I was born. Oh, I wonder why they left the first time to go back to Riordan. I don't know. I don't know. My father had a place at Riordan for a while. That is, I had a farm there but he didn't stay there long. I don't remember much about it. But when we settled on the, my grandparents had come ahead of us and we stayed with my grandparents and my mother's brother until we got our house up, I know. I remember when we moved in there. I, oh, the snow was so deep. I remember that Papa shoveled it clear down to the ground and, oh, that seemed like an awful deep ditch of snow. And later on in life I asked my mother how old I was when we moved in there and she said, you were three years old when we moved in there. And, oh, that snow was deep. This was moving into your own house. Yes, yes. We had, they had the log cabin up then and moved into, from my uncle's place. You can remember back to when you were three. I remember the, I remember moving the day we moved in there and all I remember is that it was a log cabin and the snow was so deep Papa dug it down to the ground and it was above my head.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the cabin like on the inside?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: At first it was just about a, maybe a 14 by 8, well, now I can't tell, I don't know, but it was just a log cabin, longer than it was wide. And we had one big room and we lived in that. Then Daddy put a, finally put a shed kitchen on and we slept, climbed up a ladder in the partition of the wall to get upstairs to sleep. And Papa and Mama's bed was in the front room but we can't step upstairs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kind of a loft?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, you'd call it a loft because later on when Papa built an addition on, of lumber, he built on a shed kitchen, shed shaped kitchen and one room and then he made a stairway and we had a stairway to go up, of course. And there was a floor above, a floor, you know, the ceiling and we had just one big room up there. About three beds around in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what your mother had to, you know, to do with in those first years in the cabin?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I mean as far as her cooking. We had stove and cooking utensils and chairs and things like that. We had perhaps equal to anybody else. Everybody there had about the same things to work with. We had, we were the first ones that had a sewing machine. And our sewing machine was loaned out many times to different neighbors that wanted to sew.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: And I remember Mama saying that, well, she did quite a bit of sewing for other people but she had the first sewing machine. When we moved there she had her sewing machine. But it was pretty bare living, I tell you.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I remember one thing that I did talk to you about before is the deep snows we used to have. Oh, yes. Tell me about that. Well, it's almost unbelievable when you think how deep the snow was. I remember one year we, I particularly remember, we had a deep snow and when everybody got, everybody in the neighborhood got out and broke their cars. We'd say break the roads, open the roads, went over the place that was laid out for roads and kept them open. And if you'd meet another team, those horses would just lunge in the snow like this. Then maybe rest a minute before they could get around. One team passing each way, you see. And the snow was often deep. But the neighbors all got out and broke their share of the road, went over their share of the neighborhood road and kept them open. Well, the share of the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this the part of the road that went in front of your own place? Is that how they would decide what was your share?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, it was in your neighborhood. Why, you didn't care. You would go gladly miles away if you needed to to help. If somebody didn't have a sledding team to open the road, the neighbor, your neighbors would gladly come around. But everybody helped like that. They were just good helpers. Good neighbors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did everybody have horse teams?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, yes. I've known some people, like some of those folks up on McGarry Butte and homesteading later than that didn't have horses. Well, maybe they didn't. But my grandfather had oxen. He had four head of oxen. I remember those great big yokes that drove over their necks. He drove oxen. My father never had oxen, but my grandfather had oxen. And there were some others that had oxen, too. They weren't the only family. Well, using, breaking these roads on them, this was quite a... Well, I don't remember breaking the roads with any oxen, I think. Oh, but doing this with horses, it was quite an undertaking. I mean, there was so much snow that it was really quite a bit of work to do. It would vary. And those horses would just lunge in that snow to get through there. And I think sometimes when the roads were like that, maybe we kids climbed out till they got the sled through heavy snow with the horses. I don't know. I don't just remember the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a long period of time that the winters lasted in those days around Avon?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I mean, was it... Yes, we had longer winters than now. Oh, much longer. But the timber was so dense that when it snowed up there in the wintertime, everybody, those farms now are cleared. But they were all covered with heavy timber. There were some of the larger timber that were often used for something else, I guess. But a lot of that black pine timber, oh, it was so heavy that when it snowed, we didn't hardly have any wind. Why, the snow would just come up on the fence post, stand up here and maybe tip over a little bit. And if a sled was setting out when it stormed like that, where it wasn't protected, and there was no wind, because the timber kept the wind away, I guess, there'd be maybe eight or ten inches of snow on the tongue of that sled. And here was the bare ground down under there that had just fallen down so gently that there was ground under the tongue of the sled. Well, it's very different now. Oh, for the sake of salt. Well, I think you mentioned to me one occasion when you went to a party in the slushy snow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me about that?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that story?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well,

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: an uncle of mine lived in the neighborhood, and the creek that ran through that country was called Bear Creek. And we wanted to go down to their house for Christmas, well, the whole neighborhood, or a lot of the neighbors were going to their house to spend Christmas Eve. And there had been, well, I would say maybe, I would say a good foot of snow or maybe more had been on the ground. And it came more soft snow, and then it turned to rain, and that just melted the snow and rushed those little creeks to their banks. And this, when we wanted to go that time, we thought we wondered if we should go, and Daddy said, well, we'll try. They loaded us in a bobsled, and when we got to the meadow where this creek went through the meadow, we had to cross that. And the bridge was elevated just a wee bit. And so, Papa hesitated, and he said, they debated, they debated to know whether they should go on or not. And Papa unhitched one of the horses and rode, I'd say, oh, maybe a half a block or a block to, he rode to the edge of the creek and on the bridge to see if it was safe to go across the bridge before he took the family across. Well, the butter was right up to the bridge, but the bridge seemed firm and solid, so he rode across and came on back and hitched up the team, and we went on through and it was all right. And by morning, enough of that, well, it had, I guess, quit raining. By the morning, the water had gone down enough that driving was perfectly safe anywhere then for the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember anything about the celebration at the house?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, they, at different places, at the school houses and at a few, not many of the houses. Once in a while, a group would gather and they'd just dance in the front room. And that night, we went to my aunt and uncle's house, Aunt Jenny and Uncle Frank Patterson's, and oh, I couldn't begin to tell you the number of people was there. There must have been 20 or so, maybe that many. And so, of course, if they had a carpet on the floor, they rolled it up or something, but I don't think so. I think they had a bare front room floor. And we danced. That is now I say, we danced. The elders danced. The married people. And there was one bachelor there. And I remember I asked my mother later on how old I was and she said I was eight years old. And he grabbed my hand and he said, come on, Katie, we'll be one couple in this quadrille. And I danced that quadrille with Bob Tiede, great big tall fellow. And I asked Mama after that how old I was and she said, you're eight years old that year. So that was my first dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that most of the people stayed overnight all night that night?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No. No. Most of them went home. But the creeks being so high, you would... The families, I expect there were some, our relatives maybe, there were truthy families of us that were relatives. And the elder folks never went to bed, they just made some coffee and stayed up, you know. But we stayed until daylight to see that everything was alright before we went home. I have an idea, I don't know, but I have an idea that somebody rode up to that bridge that wasn't very far from their house to see if it was safe for us to go. Well, I imagine that your father really wanted to go to the party or otherwise he wouldn't have even bothered going out on a night like that. So, he was having fun. There was nothing else for entertainment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember if there was a Christmas tree that night?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I'm just curious to know if they had a tree for Christmas. Well, now everybody didn't put up Christmas trees in their houses at that time. They had one general big tree in a church or somewhere. And that was the big occasion. I don't remember much about having Christmas trees in the house until I was older. When I was older and we had a frame house and more room, well then we always had a Christmas tree in the house. But I don't remember about that. I doubt if they had any Christmas tree in their house that night because there were a lot of people there. I say a lot of people. There wouldn't be very many people that would dance in a room this size. So, there weren't a lot of people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now there was a very small town of Avon, wasn't there?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Rather in that period of time, but it wasn't located where the present, not where the road goes now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Am I right?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No. Now let me think a minute.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: The post office was in a private house. There was a long time that there was no post office established. And anybody in the neighborhood that went to Moscow brought the mail for any of the neighbors. Moscow, I think then, was the closest post office, the nearest post office. And anybody that went to Moscow brought the mail. Now I think I'm right on that. I think I'm right on that because I think the mail came from Moscow. But after that, then there was a post office established in the Park's home. Mr. and Mrs. Park had no real young children. Their family was grown more than some of the rest of the families. And Mr. and Mrs. Park had the first post office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you been up to Avon?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, you know where Avon is now. Well, the Park's house was just up that hill south of there and was in a log cabin. And we never went in when we got the mail. We went to the window. Mrs. Park's bought us, met us at the window when we kids went for the mail and handed it out to us. She nearly always had a cookie for some of the kids, for the kids too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then there was, was that Avon post office?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: That was Avon post office and the mail was about, brought from Moscow, I think. I don't think it, it might have been Troy. Now I'm not sure about that at all. But it was brought in there. Then later on, we had fun and oh, when the snow started to go off in the spring around those great big yellow pine trees, the ground would get bare for quite a space around it. And sometimes we could get through the snow and get up to that bare ground. Oh, that was just wonderful.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: But, there was always some amusement in the neighborhood, different homes, you know, get together and have friends in. But we didn't have much to do there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have many playmates?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh yes. The families that lived around there, we had six children, the Scotts had seven children, the Greenwoods had nine children, and we were all within a half a mile of each other. Places all joined. Oh yes, we had lots of kids to play with as far as that's concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it was more playing house kind of thing than, did you have games like Run Sheep Run? Oh yes, and when it snowed, you know how they, what is this game that they track around and cross this way and that way and try to tag each other or something? What is that? What was that game?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I'm not sure. Well, we just make a great big circle in the snow and then we cut across it this way and across this way like this, that thing. And try to catch each other. Maybe we choose up sides and try to catch each other around there. I don't know. You make your own play when in those days you make your own entertainment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about responsibilities? Did your folks expect you to do your show?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: We had little chores to do. Every one of us had some certain thing to do to help keep home going. Sure, we all had our duties.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: And Mama used to tell us, every time you go out through the woodshed, you bring in an armful of wood. Make your head save your heels. Had a big wood box in the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about school?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, we had a schoolhouse and had school, yes. We generally had three months school at a time. I don't know who. I don't know just when we had a school or who was the first teacher. But I know my brother went to school one year before I started. And when I started the school, Kenneth Wilkins' grandmother, Lizzie Lunsford, was our teacher. Then I think from then on, school just went. Every year we had, I think we only had three months of school each year. And then as my brother and I got older, as we got older, some children from an adjoining school district who didn't have enough pupils to hold their district would come to our school. And we did the same thing. When they had their school, my brother and I used to ride or walk three miles down to this other district to help them hold that district. We didn't have enough pupils.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they have school at the same time, the two districts?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, no. The different times. And there would be, now this never happened in the winter. It was in the summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They would have three months at the, what did they call that district? Was it Applequist by any chance?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, down toward Applequist. That name is familiar, but that was later in years. But their school, there was a big family lived down there by their school, the Stockwills. And they used to come to Avon. And my brother and I and different ones from Avon used to go down to their school. Sometimes we rode a horse, sometimes we walked that three miles to school. So then you would get more than three months schooling by going to their school. It was an understanding, I think, between the districts for the students to do that because they didn't have enough to justify them to hold school, nor we didn't have enough either. And so they would come up to our district, some of them, and we would go down to their district.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was yours called Avon School District?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, Avon School.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how many kids were there at the school?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I'm not meaning exactly, but just more or less. Now, somewhere around here I've got one of the teachers gave us cards. And there were, there were, do you have a, is it, no, I have a picture here somewhere. The Lunsford's had Maggie and Leila and Alta and Gertrude and Fred. Then there were some younger ones. And at that time we had Charlie and Kate and Molly and Ann in our school. And then the Rowe family had ten children. They weren't all in school. But we had to have so many children to hold a district. So, now not all of the Avon kids would go from down to this other Nelson Schoolhouse or whatever it was. But we did change back and forth like that, so we helped each other, helped each other hold their district. What do you think about the virtues or the drawbacks of the one room schoolhouse for learning when you were a kid learning in that... And they taught all grades and one teacher took care of all grades.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was that like for a kid trying to learn?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Really, it was wonderful for you to get that start even. But I wonder sometimes how a teacher ever handled them. But she had, well it was reading and writing and arithmetic. That's what it was, you see. And spelling. So, it was, well, she'd have those classes regularly. With all those different grades in one, I imagine she couldn't pay too much mind to any one kid. Well, no. Not to any one child. But she could have her... She'd sign the lessons and then, well, and then hear the different lessons that were verbal and the others, the arithmetic and things like that. And then correct her papers and so on and so forth. I don't know how they ever did it. I don't know how the teachers ever handled all those classes. But they were all simple. Schoolings were simple then. Last night, I was in to my next door neighbor's here. She asked me to come in and it showed a picture of the ants. And we had one book at school. The district then furnished the books. And we had, for one of our, we had a book that we studied about ants. And I just could remember every bit of it when I saw that show the other night that Mrs. Lucas asked me in to see. We had a reader. And I don't know whether this book that we had was grammar. Oh, how I liked grammar. I don't show it now, but I used to enjoy the grammar lessons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you consider that in those days that the grown ups were strict with the kids?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, much more than now, I think. Yes, I think they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would that be different than the way they are nowadays?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: It's hard for me to even imagine. They give them too much freedom nowadays. They let them do as they please. If we wanted to go anywhere or do anything, we asked our parents and how long can we be gone and when should we be home and things like that. They had some jurisdiction over the kids in those days.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I've heard that some people say that they could ask their mothers more than their fathers. Mothers were more lenient. Maybe fathers were harder to approach. Maybe kids were a little afraid of their fathers a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that true in your family?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Not exactly in our family. That is, Mama did quite a bit of the correcting about the house and things like that.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Sometimes when we would go to bed at night we wouldn't go to sleep. It would be noisy and Mama would say, Alfred, speak to those children. That's all that had to be done.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: But it was just the same. Now listen, when our boys were little, I would put them to bed.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Is that thing registered?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yeah. And they'd want a drink. They'd want this. And finally I'd say, this is the last time I'm coming to the bedroom tonight. You go to sleep.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, I think children in those days, their parents knew where they were and knew what they were doing. And I think that there was much more training in the home than there is nowadays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know whether the hard times in 1893, whether that struck at the people at Avon too?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, sure it did. Sure it did. When we went home from school, and now this is only when my brother and I were in school, the two older ones. When we went home from school, we took off our shoes. And my mother had made kind of moccasins out of old overhaul legs or something like that for us to wear in the house. Yes, and there were a lot of people. There were some children that didn't have overshoes. And one winter we were of one of those families, and I told you before how we made Idaho overshoes. With gunny sacks, wrapped our feet in gunny sacks and went to school. Now those were called...

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Here they are. Our Idaho overshoes were called Klondike boots. And we had a square piece of gunny sack. I think I showed you when you were here. Well, you told me about that. You said it was Idaho overshoes. Yes, and we wrapped the... that was wrapped around our legs and then we had binding twine and we tied those on and wore those over our shoes. And then when we got through, they were taken off and hung up and dried. Most everybody had those Idaho overshoes, Klondike boots.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Of course there were some people that had more money than some of us did and they had their overshoes. It wasn't every family that wore those overshoes, but we were one of the families that did. It sounds like it must have been really rough on your parents especially to stick it out until things started looking good. I know it was pretty rough. We ate pretty plain food. Lots of beans and of course everybody in those days had gardens and cellars that they could... well, they could have vegetables all winter because they had good vegetable cellars. But that wasn't when we first started there. It was pretty rough, pretty rugged I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say beans and what else would be the kind of food you'd have in them early days?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, we even had pie sometimes because when they butchered they had their own shortening you see. So we had things like that, shortening. People killed their own meat, their butcher of beef. And they always had lots of pork on hand. That was a big thing. Everybody had a stalling vet and they butchered their own pigs and cured their meat. They had smoke houses where they cured the ham and all those things you see. They didn't have refrigeration and things like that. So they had smoke houses and they cured them until they would keep all summer. We don't get nice smoked ham like we used to then.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, it was pretty rugged in those days I'll tell you. But everybody in the country around us, they were practically in the same boat, you know, same circumstances as other people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, to get money of any kind, I mean to have what little money that you would need, how would your father get even that?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: We went away from home to work, went out to the Palouse country or the Moscow country and followed a thrashing machine or things like that. And when it come to keeping up the roads in the neighborhood, everybody worked. They got out and worked on the roads to keep the roads and everybody. If they wanted a new road somewhere, the neighbors put it there, you see, and kept it going and graded the roads. Maybe had different machinery to work when they wanted to grade a road. They had these great big scoopers, maybe you've seen them, that a team pulls and they hold them and slide it along until they get where they want to dump it. I've forgotten what they call them. But everybody got out and helped work to make the roads through a neighborhood. There was always a road supervisor, somebody that engineered it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he just one of the local people, one of the neighborhood? A supervisor?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I think so, as I remember it. Probably picked by the... Yes, by... well, I don't know for the... not by boat or anything, by just decision in some way. I don't remember about that at all, but I know that there was lots of road work making the roads and the neighbors all... Well, it was common for a man to get out and work on the roads. They got paid for it, and I can't tell you where the pay came from now. Maybe the county or the state. I wonder about what time of year they'd usually work on the roads.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be in the spring?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Early spring to get the roads in shape for summer. And that was when most of the road worked, but then after they got them... after the snow was gone and they got the roads in shape again, well, then there wasn't much road work done all summer, you see, unless it was where they knew they had to drain the place so the water wouldn't cross the road or something like that, or make a ditch or fill in to raise it above a place to make it... the supervisors knew what to do. And it wasn't just a few guys, it would be a whole... most of the men in the community working on it. I think that was county work. I think it was, or state work or something of the kind. And they had a supervisor, yes. You know, it seems from the families, the names of the families, that unlike most of the country through there, which was made up largely of Swedish and Norwegian people, it sounds like around Avon there were more settled Native American people. They were. They were. Until later on, a few of the Norwegians came into the neighborhood. But the Scandinavian district was south of where Deary is now. And there was a store out there. Mike Kluver had a store there. And that was where the Scandinavians settled, out south of Deary. And then Avon was White Phillips. That is, they were just different people.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: The other people that moved into the Avon area, besides this group that we talked about now, the Wilkinses, they didn't come, they weren't in any way connected with your people until Avon... Until later, until Avon was established. So they came in independently. Yes, they came independently from, oh I think out in Troy district. And I don't know where they originated. Well actually, as I recall, he came, Ken Wilkins' grandfather came in from Blooser, that way. The same way, yeah. The same way I imagine your people came. I expect so. I don't know. I don't remember about that. But most of the other people who were moving in too, they were Americans. Oh yes. Same, not Scandinavians. And then now the Axels, I don't know where they moved from, but they were considered just well educated people, you know. Class. And one of the ladies talked, well there were two of them were school teachers, this widow woman, Mrs. Hall, Mary F. Hall. I never knew her husband. He must have died before they came into the Avon country. But they came in, the Axels and Mrs. Hall. The Axels were the parents of Mrs. Hall, and she and her daughter lived with them. And they were just well educated people, but I can't tell you where they came from. They lived over west of where the Avon town was. But they were well educated people, and two teachers of them, Mrs. Hall taught our school for so many years, and then her sister, Hattie Axel, taught I think in some Palouse school or somewhere down there anyway. I think we felt she was pretty well educated, and they were well educated people, the Axels.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the people from the Cheney area as far as the educational background that they had?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: They lived ordinary like the rest of us. I don't, but the Axels had both, the women, there was one boy and he went to college at Pullman, and then there were three of the sisters and they were all teachers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about church? Did you have a community church?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, it had a community church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let's see, what was their first little church there?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: The first little church building, if I remember rightly, was in about the middle of where the cemetery now is.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Before there was a church there, I remember the first funeral there, Jasper King was killed. He was blasting stumps of danimite, and he got killed. I remember my sister Ann said, they never let her go anywhere like that, but I went with my mother and my aunt to that funeral, that's the first grave in that cemetery. The cemetery wasn't fenced or anything, and the church building was right in the center of that graveyard up there now. I can't tell you how long it was there or anything about it because it was there, I don't remember when that church was changed and moved down to Avon, that is, they made the church at Avon instead of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the whole neighborhood come to the funeral?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: A great many of them, yes, just a great many of them came.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: His mother, this Jasper King that was killed, was my Sunday school teacher for years and years, and I've got a little book that she gave each one of the pupils that learned the books of the Bible. I've still got that little book she gave me, Mrs. King.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the little book a book of Bible readings or something?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, it was just a book of little stories, children's stories, first yard stories, and it had pictures in it. It was just lovely.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the church have a denomination?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, it was just all denominations together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just a community church?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: It was just a community church, and there was one gentleman, a bachelor, that always led the singing. He had a good bass voice and he had a little tuning fork. He...dobey's hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He'd hold it next to his ear?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, he'd bite this, there was a little tuning fork, and that was supposed to give him the tone of a certain note, and from there on, but he had a good...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do that before each song?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Each song, when he'd get the tone, and he started the singing. He led the singing.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: He and his mother, well, he had a sister, and they were, oh, I'd say, long in their 30s or so like that. She was considered a maiden lady, and her brother and mother, they were the Custer's, and everybody called her Grandma Custer's. They were very nice, and they were Quakers, but they came to our church. It was just a community church. No certain religious sect at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was there any minister who would come to the church occasionally, or did you just make do without one?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did we do?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I was wondering if maybe there was a circuit ride, or if you had someone come... Well, we did later in years. Someone would come and preach, and then go from our community over to another community. Maybe he'd be gone away for a whole week, but he'd attend and have meetings at a number of churches around.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I don't remember anything much about a minister at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever have revivals that you can remember?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, yes. Yes, they had revivals. Give people opportunity to come up and say that they wanted to do different. Yes. They had revivals, sure. I think when they did that, it was ministers that came in from outside. I don't remember much about it, but I know that they did have revivals.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they ever have camp meetings that you can remember? You know what I mean by that?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes. Not in early days that I remember, but I may remember in later years that some of us even went up to some of the lakes to a camp meeting and things like that. But I don't think we had any camp meetings in our neighborhood, but we went out to where there were bigger gatherings. And I remember they had tents and had their revivals in tents. I remember that. There were two boys, young fellas, I'd say boys, two young fellas that used to come up into our neighborhood. And they were from Lewiston and they had wonderful voices and I've forgotten their names, but they were both good singers. And they visited around from different places in the neighborhood and maybe you'd stay up there for a week or two. And there were ministers that came and each family would take them for a while. There was a family minister and his wife that came to our house, Mr. and Mrs. Pullen, and they were there a week and they had church all week. And we kids didn't go to their meetings, but Papa and Mama went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then they would leave the neighborhood entirely and go on to some other places?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, they would stay with different ones in the neighborhood, maybe be in the neighborhood a month or so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ah, they might stay for a month at a time?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, in the neighborhood. I remember there was one fellow by the name of Pullen. He and his wife used to come into the neighborhood and stay. And we kids didn't go to the meetings, but Papa and Mama went. They would like to pull... No, I guess the dining room and the kitchen was all together. But here's a room where she cut the mica and then the dining room and the kitchen and then her and my bedroom was all, just three of those three rooms there. And she was, the mica that was just mined out wasn't solid like this big bunch that I just told you about. It came out just in pieces. And it was ragged edges and she just had a knife and she just cut off those ragged edges and laid out the good mica, you see. That was just mica dust that had to be cleaned up every day. But she worked in the dining room there and did that. And they just, they got it. The mica was ragged and she just had a knife and she just, supposing this was just all sticking out here and some loose, she'd just do like, kind of like this. She'd kind of trim it. To make it more even so that all this dust and stuff wouldn't go and then put that out. This is the better mica, you see. And this mica dust, of course, had to be cleaned up and just taken off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she the only cutter that they had working in the mine?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, yeah. And we were the only two women up there. All the rest of the men were, all the rest were men that worked in the mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they brought mica into her and she just took it out?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: They had a cart and a track laid back in the mica mine and they just filled out of that and brought it in and brought in the mounds for her to work on each day. And so she just kind of shaved off the edges so it was better. There might be several pieces of thicknesses here. She just kind of shaved off those rusted edges and put it into a better, for it was better mica.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was her name? Do you remember?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, I know her because she was Monica Peterson.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I went with her brother for nine years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she from Avon too?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, she was in Avon, the Avon Schoolgirls. And that was work that women should do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what about the cooking that you did? How many were you cooking for?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Was it a big... I think we had about six men maybe. I think maybe that many. Seems to me there were about that many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Three meals a day?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, I've done lots of cooking like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm just curious how long at a time did you stay up there?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, they just mined enough to work. Now I can't tell them to work out the assessment on them. That's all the time they worked. And those mines didn't operate. They would have been paid off if they would have operated off longer I guess. But of course they got to using something else and sit them, I guess. It wasn't needed so much. And I don't know the nature of that. Now some of the lunchrooms could tell you more about that than I know. But I just worked up there when they were working. I was at the Luella mine and then the one above it several miles were... ...or several up the hill further was the Moscovite. I remember one time, one evening, we from where I was working, the mine where we were working, all walked up the hill and had a dance in their dining room. Well, the dining room and kitchen all together, you know, had a dance up there. Our music was mouth organ. We walked up that hill, climbed up that hill and kind of slid back down. But you couldn't hardly keep your footing. And you asked about getting groceries up there. When we wanted groceries, there was always a team of horses that would go. Now I don't know. This is kind of blurry of me about the team of horses. But how we got them. But once we...when this girl and I were working there and we had just gone up there, we didn't have mirror or anything. So we wanted a mirror. And so the boss, Charlie Lacy, said, well you take the team and go down to Troy and get the groceries. And you do what shopping you want to do. So I've got the mirror in there now that I bought then. I paid a dollar and a half for it. And it's lovely, great being there about so long. And it's a nice one. And so we got the groceries and came home. And oh, the awfulest rainstorm and the horses weren't shot. And to come up and climb that hill with that wagon of horses. And we had groceries. We had flour and everything. And we were wet. Oh, we were wet and cold. And we drove that team up there and sometimes they'd slip, slide around in that mud going in the hill. I thought we'd never get up. And when they'd get tired pulling, we'd have to slam on the brake and block the wheels. Oh, that was an awful trip. But she and I made that trip to Troy to do some shopping that we needed to do and to bring back the load of groceries. Other than that, some of the men went. And I don't remember about where the horses came from or whether they got a... What we call the foot of the hill down in the Avon country. And they might have got his team to drive out. I don't know. I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it two horses and a wagon?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, two horses and a wagon. Uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of pay did you get for doing that?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I can't tell you. I can't remember what I got. But, oh, I don't know. It might have been a dollar a day. Something like that. It wasn't much. It might have been 50 cents a day. I don't know. It wasn't much, but it was the wages they were paying in those days. But I don't remember, really, what wages I got. Sounds like when you had to cook out there before you had a cookhouse, that must have been... Well, I think it wasn't... I don't think I had to cook out there. It was just a little shack of a stove. Two sheet iron stoves. But, so happened I didn't have to... I guess we must have bought bread or something. I don't know. I don't remember those details. But anyway, I cooked out over the shed until they got the cookhouse and room built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think you moved into Deery?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: When Earl and I were married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you and he meet, by the way?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: At a dance. And a funny thing. When I worked, I worked one time for the axel. And on Halloween, the young girl there, Alice Hall, she was a granddaughter of the axel. But on Halloween, we were peeling apples. And we were saying, look at the moon over your right shoulder and your wish will come true and things like that. And we peeled apples and they were supposed to put them around your head like this and then wherever they fall, that will be the initial...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The initial of the person you marry?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: The initial of the last name of the person you're going to marry. And on that Halloween night, I had a dream that I saw the most handsome young fellow I saw. Oh, he was handsome. And when I met Earl, that was the fellow I saw in my dream. That's a fact. Now that's a fact. You hadn't met him when you had a dream. I hadn't met him when I twirled this apple around here and all and made my wishes and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you get an initial G when you twirled the apple around?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I don't remember about that, but I... The dream. The dream that I had. I saw this young fellow at a dance. And the minute I saw Earl, I knew that was the fellow I think. Now I know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you still going with young Peterson at that time?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, at that time. Well, nine years is a pretty long courtship. I imagine even in those days... Yes, it was. But we just never got around to getting married. He finally said to me, well, 19 -8 or never, Kate, but we never got married. I went with him a little. He was a fine fellow. Schoolmate. Knew him for years and years and years. Did you and Mr. Brannis, you met at the dance and it sounds like you kind of might have fell for each other right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Earl say about that one time?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, he told me afterwards. Now, we've talked about this since when he first went up to the home. We kind of went over things like this. And he told me once that, no, when he first met me, he never thought of marrying me. And then when he wanted to kid me later, after we'd gone together a number of years, several years, I had to tell everybody else I had to marry her to get rid of her. He's quite a joker. Full of fun. But when I met him, I knew that that was a fellow that I had seen in my dreams. I just knew that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go together for very long before you went?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, I don't remember. A couple of years, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get chivalry?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes. No, no. Instead of having a chivalry, they gave us a shower. When we came back from our wedding trip, they gave us a shower at Derry and took up a collection and raised $50 and we bought a stove with us. And Chuck Wells, you know who Chuck Wells is, wasn't at the dance. But the next time he saw Earl, he took a dollar out of his pocket and he said, I didn't get to put my contribution in, so you take this, he said. So he gave Earl a dollar. All the rest of the fellows, I guess, were giving a dollar that we bought our stove with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you moved right into Derry then?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yeah, we moved right into Derry when we were married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was he working for Potlatch at the time?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, he wasn't working for the Potlatch. He was running the livery barn and a pool hall. He and another fellow, he and Frank Holbrook. Frank wasn't in on the livery barn, but Frank was in with him on the pool hall. They had a pool hall there and Earl run the livery barn and he's a veterinarian and he practiced.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a vet then?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, he has taken care of lots of the farmers, hogs and horses and cows. So they always call him Doc Grandis, that's where he got his name, Doc Grandis.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he just come into the area at that time when Derry started up or had he been there before?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: He came to Derry from Helmer. His people were located up at Helmer and there are some of those in the picture there. And he was with them until they moved down to Derry. He came out when they were still at Helmer. Then they all moved down to Derry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He came out from?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: From the east. I've forgotten what state now. Iowa, I guess. Well, you mentioned Chuck Wells and I just got to ask you some of those recollections of Wells' family. I've danced with Chuck and Roy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Danced with Chuck and Roy. Now I was going to... in their home, I guess, or either in their home or...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would it be?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I've danced with both of them.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: My father and mother had the whole Wells family at our house for dinner more than one Sunday and Mary carried me in her arms. I was small enough that she could pick me up and carry me. And they've been to dinner in our folks' home. So they're pretty good friends with your parents. Those people were treated just as any white person when they came. In early days, nobody shunned those darkies at all. Not at all. They were in different families' homes. I've been in their homes... well, when we lived at Derry, we went up there one night and had a dance. Joe was drinking and enjoying himself, but we danced at their house. But now that was... I think that was after Mary was gone from there. I think the Derry bunch went up there and I think just Joe and his wife, Lou, were there. Because I don't remember ever going after when Mary was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say in the early days, do you think it changed then as years went on?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh yes. Oh yes. Then when Mary's child was born, I don't know how many people at Derry went up to see her right away because they were all curious. Because they said they tell me that a little darkie child is white when it's born. I didn't go up and I don't know the result or anything about it, but I know people were curious. And then it turns dark. Well, that's what some of them said, but now I don't know about that at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you think that the attitude changed and they really weren't as well -existed as time were they?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, different people come in and then they never thought of the wells. Like, they didn't take them in like the early day people did. That is, our family and several that I know of just visited with them the same as we visited with white folks. Well, they came in with the Wells boys, you know, who were carpenters there and took the name of Wells because they had been, I think they were the Wells' father's slaves. I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't I tell you that before? Have you ever been to Wells' where? Have you?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, yes. They came there and my father thought a great deal of the Wells boys, they were blacksmiths, and Joe and Mary came, the family came there with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you remember Lou as being like? Joe's wife?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I don't know. She was well thought of as far as I know and I don't remember anything about her housekeeping or anything, but it seems to me that I remember her once kind of dancing a little dance step in our dance hall up there or something. Now it seems to me I remember that, but I won't say sure. Well, there was a bachelor that lived out there in, you know, it's between Derry and Avon. It's nearer Derry than Avon. And Phil Flemons was a, I don't know whether I should tell you this or not.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, anyway,

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I don't know if I should.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: You don't have to use a name if you don't want to. Well, let's say there was a fellow that had married through a matronial paper and he and his wife didn't get along. And he threatened her some way and she, I was away from home when this happened, but the girls said, my tour about it, and his wife came to our house to stay a while. And then she went back and I don't know how he threatened again in some way and another neighbor had come to get her to take her away or take her back home now, which it was, I don't know. And he was there and he shot her and then she,

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: no. I better not tell, anyway, he shot himself in the home then. He knew he'd done what he'd done and he shot himself in the home. And they didn't know what all had happened there and a bunch of fellows from Derry went out there armed to take him into custody or something. And he had farmed, he was farming and he had planted grain right up to close to the house. And they were creeping up to the house and Chuck Welch was one of the fellows that was looking, spying to this place. And of course this is a joke about Chuck and they said that the door was open and he creeps up kind of close and the door had been open quite a while and evidently the chickens went in and the chicken jumped down off the table. And then when they said, Chuck, turn white, he was so frightened. The joke is about niggers, you know, that if they get frightened, they'll turn white. So that was the joke about that. I was going to ask you about that killing because Kent Wilkins told me that you would be the person who would most likely know the truest account of what had happened. Because I've heard two different accounts of that killing. That this man, what I heard was that the woman was kind of, she was being threatened by him quite a bit after they were married. And I don't know about him, he married her through a matrimonial paper and she had been threatened until she was afraid to stay there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well didn't she come down to stay at your place?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, she was at our house for a while. We were young, I wasn't at home, I was away from home when this happened, but she came down and stayed at our house for a while. I don't know how long and then Billy Bauer, I think, took her home, finally, and then he shot her, you know. He shot her when she was getting out of the wagon. The buggy that he had taken her home in. And then, of course, shot himself. And Earl was one of the groups that went down with guns and crap around there to find. And, of course, they didn't know that he might shoot them. They didn't know he was dead, you see. And so, but that was the joke, that the door was open and when a chicken dropped, jumped down off the table, Chuck was near the door and they said he pretty near turned white. Frightened himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, you mentioned that the first few years of your life you were living with your aunt and uncle?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, Uncle and Aunt Florence and Uncle Ruth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, how did that work?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: You had your folks and the kids, you and your brother were staying. Well, at that time, they had, I said something about nine children in their family. Well, that was the family that grew up, but at the time that we stayed with them, they, unless they, they had about three and we had three. So that wasn't much of a family, you know. And we didn't all have beds to sleep on. We had pallets on the floor sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was their house a cabin and a small one too? Or did they have more?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I'm thinking it was quite something for them to put up your family too, so you... Well, it was. But they had trunnel beds for kids that pushed under their beds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they called trunnel beds?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Trunnel beds.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I think every home had a trunnel bed for the kids to sleep in and that bed, they'd pull it out from under the big bed and our bedsteads were homemade.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Our bedsteads. Maybe there were lots of the neighbors that had bedsteads that were bought. But my grandfather was a carpenter and he made our bedsteads. We had bedsteads. Nearly, well, for a number of years we didn't have anything but homemade bedsteads and slats and straw ticks. We had no bed springs. Slats and straw ticks. And then most everybody had ducks or some way they'd raise and they had a feather bed. And we had feathers for pillows, of course. But you got along with most anything those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was this family, how were they related to yours? Was it your father's or mother's?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, when I say we stayed with my uncle, he was my mother's brother. He was a Greenwood. And there were three families of Greenwoods that moved into that locality.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All of them homesteaded?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, all of them homesteaded. Took up homes. Homesteaded, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in these early years there, did many of these families that first moved in from the, that you knew of, Nate from Cheney and around there, did many of them leave?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, they stayed for years and years. And one of the first families that moved away from Avon and went to Spokane was the Graybill family. And there wasn't a dry tear in that church the last Sunday they were there. Everybody hated to see them go so. And they just wept. There wasn't a dry tear. Oh, we hated to see them. And I was old. No, I guess when they left I must have been eight or ten years old. Boy, that's surprising to me that so many people could, you know, would stay and there wasn't a lot of turnover in those early years. No, not in those few early years. When somebody came to the neighborhood, well, the early settlers, it seems like the early settlers that went there and took homesteads just stayed. And there were just quite a few went in there when that little plot of land was thrown open for settlement. Was thrown open for settlement.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was very hard on your mother?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, I do. They didn't have things to do with like we do nowadays. And we had washboards, they washed all their clothes by hand. And they didn't have things to work with.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: There were good many girls in that school for years there.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: We had about two good dresses. Or one really good dress, that is calico. And they were washed over the weekend and we wore that same dress through the whole term of school. Three months. But mom would wash them every weekend and have them just shining clean and iron, starched and ironed so nice. And everybody was in that same boat. They didn't have changes of garments. Maybe some of them did have two dresses that were fit to wear to school, but most of them were just about all in the same boat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about going to town? Did your mother get a chance to go to the city at all and a change of pace?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No. No. Well, the women did go to town. They did go to town. But you should see how they had to travel. They had big hats and the dust was so thick that they wore dusters over their dresses and big veils over their hats. Because you drive along there, let your horses trot a little bit and the dust just rolled up. Oh, we hated that dust. Oh, how we hated that dust. But people didn't come and go as much as they do nowadays. They went to town maybe. Papa used to go to town and put in a year's supply of flour. Stack it in the upstairs. They put in a year's supply of flour. Stack it in the upstairs. A lot of people did that. Put in their flour for the winter and things like that so they didn't have to travel so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everybody had teams of horses and some of them didn't have any different than a big wagon but some of them had, what did they call them, hacks?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Kind of a spring wagon that had springs on it. A little more comfortable to ride in than an old lumber wagon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But if your mother was going to get the chance to go to town, would town be Troy or Moscow that she would get to go to?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: She didn't get to go very much. It was daddy that went and did the shopping and she stayed home more. I won't say about the rest of the neighborhood but I think it was principally the men folks that went and got the staples of groceries and things that they needed. I remember more than once that my father brought home yardage enough to make my mother a new dress or something like that. He'd select the material and she didn't go as much as lots of the ladies did. But it was nothing unusual for the men folks to select. Nobody bought ready -mades. Even the men's shirts, most of them we made. My mother always made her own dresses. When we kids got older and she had a nice black dress, everybody in those days had to have a good black dress. I bet she wore hers for ten years. But she took good care of them. They had what they called a dust ruffle. Their dresses were cleared down to the ground. They had a little ruffle that just came down, just sat down enough below this edge of your hem that protected you from getting dragging on the ground. They were that long. You've seen long dresses now. Oh, I've seen long dresses but I never knew about the dust ruffle. Well, they put the dust ruffle on their good wool dresses, their better dresses, not their washable dresses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they as long, the washable dresses?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh yes, oh yes, oh yes, they were long. You didn't see a woman on with a dress that wouldn't almost, any dress would sweep the ground. Then they'd hold them up and they'd go out in the dust, you know, when they walked out in the dust.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she would wear the long dress working at home?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, they never wore short dresses in those days. The women's dresses were all floor length. It seemed kind of inconvenient for them doing housework. I know it, but they wore them, no, the women's dresses were all floor length in those days.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, you didn't see a woman with, that you could see her ankles in those days. Their dresses come clear to the ground, clear to the floor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did every woman have to have one good black dress?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, now maybe everyone didn't, but it was considered. Well, nowadays we think we have to have one good black dress because there's occasionally a time that you want a black dress, but they, maybe some of the people that were in better circumstances didn't think of things that way, but my mother and my aunts in the family, they always felt they had one good dress, a good winter dress and one good long summer dress. Then the other rappers, they just called them rappers, and they were more simple, but they were floor length, however.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were both the winter and summer dresses black?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh no, no, no, they just had one good black dress. One good black dress. One good black dress.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the difference in occasions? I'm sure you'd wear a black dress to a funeral, but what other occasions would you wear a black dress?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: They were considered the dressiest dress in those days. I've made dolls, and different people have given me different old, old things to make up for my dolls, and you'd be surprised. There were black beads, beaded stuff, oh as white as my fingers, oh the dresses were trimmed in, beautiful things, but every lady was considered, she was well dressed if she had a nice black dress that she could put on for special occasions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about visiting back and forth among the women? Was there much chance, much time for visiting with your neighbors?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, I think it was more in helpfulness that the women did the visiting. They'd go so and so in such a place and help a lady do something for that day or something like that. Well, I'll tell you what I think about their visiting. Most of it was done on Sundays. There always was, everybody had somebody go home with them for Sunday dinner, come go home for Sunday dinner. And there wasn't as much visiting back and forth, and no telephone, she couldn't do a lot of gossiping. But, and if there was anybody sick in the neighborhood, there were always just several that were so very good to help out, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would that mean? That would mean going to the home and taking care of the work that the sick woman couldn't do?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, for the day, for the day, and then go home at night or something like that. And if they felt that a neighbor needed help, yes. But each family was pretty self -sufficient, I tell you. They just didn't, I remember one time there was, well, it was one of the neighbors died, and my mother, and they had a family of girls, and my mother went over and stayed with them one day, well, a day or two.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: And she came home one night, and we kids were fighting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said, aren't you ashamed when these people are having such a hard time?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: They've lost their mother. She was trying to shame us out of what we had been scrapping and fighting amongst ourselves. She must have done it too, I'll bet. She must have felt bad about it. Well, sure she did. She said, the sadness is in their home, and then here she said, you children are fighting. She never said kids, and never used that word. That was a bad word with my mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that they got together in health, besides the sickness, what would be the kinds of reasons that women would have to get together? Would they work together in sewing and that kind of thing?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Not sewing, but I think they once in a while used to in canning or doing something like that, or butchering, they'd go and help. I know that when a neighbor would butcher several pigs and all, why, other neighbors would come in maybe with a sausage grinder, and some would cut up sausage and grind it, and they'd work, and first thing you know they'd have all that meat cut and ready, put down in brine and all. Yes, neighbors would help each other that way. But this was as much the men as women getting together. Oh yes, oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the women didn't get together themselves particularly?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, no, not particularly, no. But no, the men neighbors were neighborly too, they helped and things like that.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I was thinking of the men going off to work in the harvests, that must have made it more difficult for the families. Yes, and when they did, the cows wouldn't come home and the pigs would get out of the fence. And I know we kids, always when daddy goes away something happens. And I've heard other women say the same thing. It seemed like, my neighbor next door said it seemed like when the men folks went away there was always something happened.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, the women were more stay at home than they are nowadays, much more.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, by the time when you stop to think that they did all, most of them did all the sewing for their own family. They even made men's shirts and some of them even made men's red flannel underwear and things like that I know. And by the time you do that and then keep your housework up and your kids in line and washed and cleaned, you didn't have too much time to visit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When children were born in the neighborhood, was it usually without doctors or midwives?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: It was without doctors and there was generally some lady in the neighborhood who was just a good hand at waiting and they were considered welcome to come and help. But, no, the neighbors did help each other. If there was a child born, some of the neighbors would come in and that and the husband took care of it. I know that that's the way it was at our house and there was two different ladies in our neighborhood who were considered just good midwives. And one or the other of them would come, go to a place where a child was being born and help them. Of course, now I know the only ones I remember of my sisters being born, I remember when they were born and a lady who we called Grandma Hutchie came with her white, she had a black skirt and a white blint blouse on. And she was just, oh, just ironed and beautiful and she came and was with Mama when our last two children, no, when the next of the last one was born. Then when the last one was born, that was seven years between the former one that had been born, and an aunt. They just picked up our kids. My brother took a sleigh and took all our little kids from our house over to my aunt's house and brought my aunt back. And she and my father delivered my last sister and they shut the kitchen door and I had to go ahead with the dishwashing and the work in the kitchen. Because, but, so, and then they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the other kids at home too or were they down at your aunt's house?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: They went, they took them all to the, when they brought my aunt over, they then... Excuse me a minute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sister, last sister being born, you doing the dishes in the kitchen?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, they, we were living at that time. We had moved in with my mother's father and mother to take care of them. And, but, and we, they lived with us quite a while, but they had moved after we took care of them, was in their house and took care of them for a number of years. Then they moved to Moscow with a brother of my mother's, an uncle, and so then we were in that house alone and had the house to ourselves. But one of the aunts came over and she and my father did the delivering and took care of the baby and, as I say, shut the kitchen door and there never was a sound or anything going on. Of course I was pretty curious, but I knew I had to stay in the kitchen. And then when, when, after this, well, I think that the children only stayed at Aunt Dolly's house just during the few hours, maybe they were, I think they were home that night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when my third sister, when they came in, they said, what do you think?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: We got a new girl, another little girl. She says, just another one to buy shoes for. Showed you how poor people felt. Just another one to buy shoes for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old was the sister of yours that said that?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, I think she was about seven years old or maybe eight. But we tease her about that now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And still tease her about it?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, we still tease her about it. Just another one to buy shoes for. Because we were, we were caution, don't you wade in a puddle of water. You keep your shoes nice. We were reprimanded pretty strongly if we abused our wearing of our shoes especially. Must have been pretty easy to get, to get them fouled up because the, because the country was pretty rugged. I mean, you know, your shoes you could. Oh, pretty rugged. I had one aunt and she loved, she pinched her feet. She wanted small feet. And she bought a new pair of shoes and had me break them in. Oh, I felt like a queen with high heels on. Boy, but she scolded me because I got them muddy. But I, she let me break her shoes in for her. I don't see where you could go in the spring, let's say, without getting your shoes muddy in the country. Why, you couldn't. Because it was, there was mud everywhere. Oh, the mud was terrible because the roads weren't graded in those days. The mud was just awful. And in the summer when you drive along with your horses trotting real fast, oh how the dusted fog up. Boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
