words: Well, did you, did you usually wear boots over the shoes in that kind of muddy weather? Did you have any?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No, we didn't have anything to put on in the summertime. That was just winter protection. And we had most of the size over shoes. But there was one winter I remember that we wore our feet tied in gunnock tacks for, we didn't have over shoes that year. No, we didn't have any. But your regular shoes weren't high heels. I mean, what you would wear as a kid. Oh no, that's why I was so proud to break in a pair of high heels for my aunt. Boy, I felt smart wearing the high heels to break them in. You know, talking about childbirth, I have heard that women were supposed to stay in bed for ten days. Ten days. That seems like a pretty long time, lying flat on your back after you had a kid. That was the medical law in those days. And we, in later years, I had my father's uncle married a German girl. And the third day she got up and did the washing. And we just always thought, my gosh, Aunt Laura washed and her baby only three days old. But that was the law in those days, the medical law for women, that they should stay in bed ten days when a child was born. But now they don't anymore. The hospital here, they get you right up and out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before the child was born, was a woman supposed to take it easy or very easy? Or could they work right up until the end?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, they worked right up to the end. Just did their work right up to the end. I don't think they might, within their selves, have been careful about some way, but most of the women just worked right up to childbirth. Just doing the regular work, as they always did.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Why that?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Because they were supposed to stay in bed for ten days. I know that. Somebody told me that was the only rest they got, so it's probably better they stay in rest during that time. I've heard more than one woman say that. The only time she got to rest.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: No matter how bad things were. That's it. That's it. Well, this seems to me to be a good example of maybe a time when divorce would have been the solution. Well, yes, but this fellow, old Clemens, was kind of an erratic fellow. We didn't any of us feel comfortable around him. Although, my mother and we girls went over there one time to visit. This was before he married his wife, and he was still a bachelor. We went over to see him, and he asked us to sing. My mother said afterwards, she said, I was afraid to say no. Afraid not to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was his reputation for having a bad temper a bit of a moody person?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, he killed a neighbor's cow or stuck her with his pitchfork once because dishonoriness. He was not a lackable fellow at all. Yes, he used to come to my grandfather's house a certain morning every day of the week to play cards with Grandpa. They were good friends, and each one tried to beat the other, of course. But we were all of us a little bit afraid. I remember one time when I was working for Mrs. Berry, and we had, and Phil Peterson's two sisters and I had gone from Berry's over somewhere to visit a neighbor. We were going home at night, and we were scared to death to go by old Phil Clemens' house. We were just afraid he might... We were scared, really.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the marriage last very long?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I can't tell you that. Not too long, I think, because I don't remember how many years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she a nice woman?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, she was very nice. That is, she was capable. She was very plain, very ordinary, but a good cook and a good housekeeper.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: So that must have been... That was an awful shock to people to think that such a thing happened in our little town, near our little town of Derry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she had been staying with, was it with your sisters that she had been?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I was working away from home. I wasn't home. And my sisters were home, and she was with them. That is, there were three girls. One girl was a real small girl at that time, but there were two teenagers' girls at home, and she went down and stayed with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they say that she was afraid for her life?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes. I wonder why she left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder if he'd, had he said he would kill her or something like that?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, I'm sure he had threatened her.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When are you ever going to show or turn those on, let anybody hear them?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, well, no. The idea with the... of McGowan and their position in Derry. I know that he laid out the town there and was kind of an upper crust in the beginning. Yes, they were. But you were telling me that they didn't come to the dances that people had in those early days around Derry. Not so much. Occasionally they would, but not so much. But they did have this one dance at their home, and the two couple of us were invited up there. And it had a pleasant evening. It just was in a home or maybe a room a little bit larger than this, maybe, where we danced. But, oh, they were lovely people. They were good people. We all liked them. Of course, they were considered the elite of the town. And they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a surprise to you, folks, to be invited up to the house?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Very much. We said they want some good dancers.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I'd hate to have any of their friends hear that. Oh, I don't think any of their friends are still alive. They've been gone for so long. Yes, they have. They have. We met one of the fellas in Spokane at a park one time where they had a celebration of some of the people who lived in Derry. They were having a - In Spokane. In a park in Spokane. They were having a, oh, it was John and Eunice Dury's 50th wedding anniversary. They had it in a park in Spokane, and Earl and I were there. And there was one fellow that we couldn't figure out who it was or something. A well -dressed fellow. And finally we found out who it was, and it was Hugh Henry. And he looked at Earl and he said, I've been wondering who you were. I thought you were some - you looked like some college professor. And Earl said he felt quite puffed up about that. Oh, this was some time back, I imagine. Oh, yes, yes. Quite a ways back. They had their 50th wedding anniversary just six months after Earl and I had our 50th. And we happened to be in Spokane and went. They celebrated out in a park. Well, you told me, I think you said that they had - that they did have a girl working in the house and she didn't - she couldn't - Oh, that was in Derry. And they had a maid, a girl who did housework for them, had home help. And of course we laughed at it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We laughed at it because we said we sat in there like, what is it, a Cinderella, not a Cinderella?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Cinderella, that's right. She sat off in another room where she could look in the C in the door, you see, where we were dancing. And she just sat there in that chair. She wasn't asked to dance at all, you see. She was a maid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know her? Was she local?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, yes, she was one of the - I mean, she probably wasn't that different from anybody else. Oh, no, only that she was a girl that did housework.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: I think you said that you felt that you felt like - You said. Well, I can't remember exactly where you put it, but it's - I think you said that, you know, you felt like you'd be at ease with her or she should be dancing. Yes, more - something like that. But she sat and watched the rest of us dance, I know. I don't just remember the remark I made at that time. Well, it's just striking because mostly, you know, I know that all the small towns had - What is the character that - go ahead and say what you're going to say. I think they have a high status. Yes, sure, sure. And that's different. Well, that's the way this family and their - we always felt that they were - and we always felt that they felt above us. And that's alright. Okay.

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Well, let me just check and see if there's anything else. Oh, I guess I want to - you know, you said that you worked - that you did other - other cooking, other places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do other jobs besides this one in the cooking that you did at the mine?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Other times were you, you know - Yes, I cooked on thrashing machines. And my sister and I, when Billy Deal and I can't think of the other fella's name, did some logging out on the potlatch. And they had a slide up the hill that they took their logs up the hill. We cooked for the crew out there. My sister and Earl worked out there. And the fella that my sister was doing this worked out there. And my sister Anne and I did the cooking at the cookhouse. And there was one family that came out there and spent the summer, the mother and four children, and paid their board just for a lark. And one of them lives here in town now. She's our - was our boys' school teacher. Grew up to be one of our boys' school teachers here in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They came out and lived - just lived to live in the woods?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Out there just for a month for a vacation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they eat with the men?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: They didn't eat with the men. They ate after the men were through. They had the same food and all, but they ate after the men were through. The family came in and ate. Well, were they - were you folks all working for potlatch in this job, or was it a gypo kind of - Well, it was for two men. Billy Deal and I - and I can't - and Hill. Hill was the other man's name. Deal and Hill. And the men out there were selling the logs and getting them ready. And then they were down in the - on the potlatch flat, you know, where the river was. And they were getting those logs up to the hill, pulling them up there with a - I can't express my thoughts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Donkey, maybe?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Oh, machinery called donkey. Yes, something on that order. They were taking the logs up the hill. It seems to me - I don't know what it was. Maybe it was a certain kind of timber, like telephone poles or something. I'm not sure about that. But anyway, they were - it was Deal's and Hill's camp. And my sister and I were doing the cooking there in the cookhouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember very well the impact on the country that potlatch coming in had? I mean, it changed things quite a bit when it came in, didn't it?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yes, it was a big change because they got a different - before - no, I didn't see now. I don't know how to word that. Their camps weren't - the woods camps weren't as nice before as that. They were more crummy than nice camps like it was after the potlatch came in. Seems to me - There was a big influx of - a bunch of new people coming in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What they call them?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: IWW's. Oh, yes. And for a while, the overheads, that is the men who were scalers and things like that, carried a gun all the time. But that didn't last too long. I kind of had the idea that when they first came in, you know, in 1925 and 1906, that they created a lot of opportunity for local people to get jobs and sell hay and that kind of thing. That's what I've been told. Well, I don't know. I don't remember, don't know how to word what I'd want to say here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The IWW's, weren't they?

speaker: Kate Price Grannis
words: Yeah, well, I think they were kind of later. They were - they got sort of around 1915 and 1917, these strikes. Uh -huh. But there were some - I don't know what the difference was, but the one thing that they did say is they made them clean up their camps and have them decent for men to live in.
